title: Why Deep Learning Needs GPUs
concept: Deep learning needs GPUs because training spends much of its time on huge matrix operations made of many similar calculations, and GPUs are built to run lots of that parallel math at once.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can quantization be a practical first compression step for deployment?

options.text: Because lower-precision weights often reduce memory use and can improve speed on the same model.
Because it removes the need for GPUs entirely.
Because it turns every task into supervised learning.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Lower-precision number formats help because moving and computing with huge piles of numbers is one of deep learning’s central costs.

cards.title: One person cannot flip every page fast enough
cards.body: Training a neural network is like repeatedly updating enormous stacks of pages, except each “page” is full of numbers. A CPU is great at many general jobs, but deep learning often asks for the same simple math over and over on huge batches. That is where GPUs shine.

cards.title: GPUs are built for wide parallel math
cards.body: A GPU has many smaller compute cores designed to handle lots of similar operations in parallel. Deep learning relies heavily on matrix multiplications and other tensor operations where the same kind of arithmetic happens across thousands or millions of values at once, so the hardware and the workload match well.

cards.title: Many burners for the same recipe
cards.body: If one chef must cook 1,000 identical pancakes on one burner, breakfast takes forever. A kitchen with many burners can cook many pancakes at the same time. GPUs are like that wide kitchen: not always the best at every task, but excellent when the work can be repeated across many items in parallel.

cards.title: A batch of images becomes one giant math job
cards.body: Suppose a vision model trains on a batch of images. Each layer turns those images into large arrays of numbers, then multiplies weights and activations again and again. Instead of handling one tiny multiplication after another, the GPU can push many of them through together, which is why deep learning training is so often GPU-centered.

cards.title: A GPU is not magically best for every workload
cards.body: A common myth is that GPUs are simply “faster computers.” Really, they are faster for certain patterns of work. Tiny models, small batches, or tasks with lots of branching logic may not benefit nearly as much. The advantage appears when the job contains lots of similar math that can run side by side.

cards.title: General-purpose brains vs. wide math engines
cards.body: Both CPUs and GPUs matter, but they are optimized for different styles of work.

left.text:
Excellent at varied control logic
Handles many different system tasks
Less ideal for massive repeated matrix math

right.text:
Built for many similar operations at once
Strong match for tensor and matrix workloads
Often the workhorse for training deep networks

exercises.prompt: Why do GPUs often help deep learning so much?

options.text: Because neural networks use many repeated matrix-style calculations that can be run in parallel.
Because GPUs automatically remove the need for training data.
Because every AI task is really a graphics problem.

exercises.explain: Correct. The core idea is hardware-workload fit: lots of repeated math spread across many values.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The biggest advantage of GPUs in deep learning comes from doing many similar numeric operations side by side.
exercises.explain: True. Parallel execution across large tensors is the main reason GPUs became so central to modern deep learning.

exercises.sentence: GPUs shine when the workload contains lots of ___ work that can happen at the same time.
exercises.explain: Exactly. Deep learning gains a lot when many similar operations can run in parallel.

exercises.prompt: Which task sounds most naturally suited to a GPU?

options.text: Training a deep vision model that repeatedly multiplies huge batches of activations and weights.
Renaming three files with different custom rules.
Writing a short project memo with many branching decisions.

exercises.explain: Right. Large repeated tensor math is the classic GPU-friendly pattern.

exercises.prompt: A team is training a large image classifier. Each step involves multiplying big batches of activations by large weight matrices thousands of times. Why is moving from CPU-only training to a GPU likely to help most?

options.text: Because the GPU can run many similar matrix calculations in parallel, matching the structure of the training job.
Because GPUs make labels unnecessary during supervised learning.
Because GPUs turn every deep model into a linear regression model.
Because GPUs eliminate all memory limits regardless of model size.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Deep learning became GPU-driven because the main workload is enormous repeated numeric computation that maps well onto wide parallel hardware.
